serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged honey grass jelly drink is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to one standard can or bottle as sold in Singapore convenience stores and supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Grass Jelly Drink (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged honey grass jelly drink (cr_id D05010023). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged honey grass jelly drink. GI estimated at 52 based on analogous sugar-sweetened herbal beverage with similar sugar content (~9g/100g predominantly sucrose/fructose from honey). Honey has a published GI of approximately 55–58 (University of Sydney GI Database); diluted sugar-sweetened beverages with comparable sugar concentrations cluster around GI 50–55. A GI of 52 falls in the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification system. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (9.2g/100g × 250g = 23g) / 100 = 11.96, rounded to 11.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.0g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage: despite low sodium (30mg/100g), the 9.0g/100g sugar content in a liquid form exceeds the 5g/100g SSB threshold and independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines on added sugar and cardiovascular outcomes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.0g sugar/100g and zero dietary fibre causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: This packaged honey grass jelly drink is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: the 9.0g/100g sugar content with zero fibre in liquid form results in rapid glucose absorption and acute postprandial blood glucose spikes; plain unsweetened grass jelly (cincau) without added honey or sugar is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar from honey and sweeteners contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and metabolic risk factors for CVD
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of this sugar-sweetened beverage (9.0g sugar/100g) is inadvisable for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, as habitual liquid sugar intake is associated with elevated triglycerides, weight gain, and worsened cardiometabolic risk factors per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar content negates any potential anti-inflammatory benefit from grass jelly extract
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Despite grass jelly's traditional use as a cooling herbal remedy, this packaged version contains 9.0g added sugar/100g with zero fibre and no confirmed anti-inflammatory active compounds at meaningful concentrations; plain unsweetened grass jelly may be preferable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Grass Jelly Drink (Packaged) is a ready-to-drink sweetened herbal beverage popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is made from grass jelly (known as cincau in Malay or xian cao in Chinese), which is derived from the dried and processed stems and leaves of Platostoma palustre (formerly Mesona chinensis), a plant in the mint family. The grass jelly extract is combined with honey and water to produce a lightly sweet, mildly herbal-flavoured beverage with a characteristic dark brown colour. Packaged versions are commercially produced and sold in cans or bottles in convenience stores, supermarkets, and hawker centres throughout Singapore. The drink is low in calories (37 kcal/100g) and low in sodium (30mg/100g), but contains 9.0g of sugar per 100g — predominantly from honey and added sweeteners — with zero dietary fibre. This sugar profile classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, it is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its high liquid sugar content, which causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation and independently raises blood pressure risk. The grass jelly component is traditionally regarded as a cooling food in Chinese herbal medicine, but the added sugar in packaged versions negates any potential anti-inflammatory or glycaemic benefit. The beverage is vegetarian, halal-certified in most commercial formulations, gluten-free, and dairy-free. A typical serving is one 250 ml can or bottle, providing approximately 92.5 kcal and 23g of carbohydrates.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010023) with confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are as reported by HPB. GI is an analogous estimate documented with approved source basis.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this is a carbohydrate-based sweetened herbal beverage with no protein-contributing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — the product contains no fat-contributing ingredients by formulation. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB — the grass jelly extract in packaged commercial form contributes negligible fibre at the concentrations used. calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this packaged beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D05010023 as of 2026. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on honey GI (~55–58, University of Sydney GI Database) and comparable sugar-sweetened herbal beverages; per the University of Sydney GI classification system, GI ≤55 is classified as low GI; gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. No direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-16. is_vegan set to false as honey is an animal-derived ingredient. Dietary tags low_sodium and low_calorie from input cultural data are preserved in classification context.
